title: He Kākano Ahau: I Am a Seed - Exploring Ngāti Kahungunu Identity through the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore their role as community members through the lens of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a significant waterway that flows through our rohe. The stream's name references the kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks, and its history reflects Ngāti Kahungunu's relationship with this taonga - from traditional food gathering sites to modern environmental challenges.
learningObjectives: Analyze how the historical significance of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream shapes contemporary community identity
Investigate and document current environmental challenges facing the stream using social inquiry methods
Collaborate with local kaumātua to understand traditional practices of stream guardianship (kaitiakitanga)
Develop and present a community action plan that integrates mātauranga Māori with modern conservation approaches

activities.title: Whakapapa of the Waterways
activities.description: Students begin by mapping the Mangakōtukutuku Stream's journey through our community, identifying historical sites of significance (pā sites, traditional fishing grounds, gathering places) and creating a visual timeline of the stream's relationship with Ngāti Kahungunu

activities.title: Mātaki, Whakarongo, Kōrero - Observe, Listen, Speak
activities.description: Working with local kaumātua, students conduct water quality testing while learning traditional methods of assessing stream health. They document both scientific measurements and mātauranga Māori perspectives through video journals

activities.title: Voices of the Community
activities.description: Students interview diverse community members (farmers, local iwi representatives, conservationists) about their perspectives on the stream's importance and challenges, practicing active listening and respectful questioning techniques

activities.title: He Rautaki Māori - Our Strategy
activities.description: In small groups, students develop community action proposals that blend traditional knowledge with modern solutions, creating digital presentations that include specific actions, timelines, and identified community partners

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated environmental reports and historical records
Local kaumātua and environmental experts from Te Taiwhenua o Heretaunga
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' monitoring data and tools
Digital storytelling equipment and water testing kits from the college's science department

tasks: Research portfolio documenting historical and contemporary relationships with the stream
Multimedia presentation proposing a culturally-grounded community action plan
Reflective journal demonstrating understanding of kaitiakitanga principles

nzcAlignment: Identity, Culture, and Organization: Understand how cultural practices vary but reflect similar purposes
Place and Environment: Understand how people interact with natural and cultural environments
Continuity and Change: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
The Arts in New Zealand Cultures: Understand how the arts reflect and reinforce identity
